speaker: Arya
en: John, how is it going with your research on water hyacinth?

speaker: John
en: Well, I went on a field trip on my own and collected the primary data.

speaker: John
en: After I came back, I also referred to the published data.

speaker: Arya
en: So what do you know about this plant?

speaker: John
en: Water hyacinth grows in tropical countries.

speaker: John
en: It has beautiful purple-blue flowers, but everybody hates it.

speaker: Arya
en: Why is that?

speaker: John
en: Water hyacinth, as the world's worst invasive aquatic plant, has attracted significant attention.

speaker: John
en: Millions of these plants grow in rivers and lakes and have brought huge environmental and cultural problems to the local people.

speaker: John
en: Farmers cannot travel in boats on the water, and thus cannot fish in it.

speaker: John
en: In addition, heavy free-floating mats of water hyacinth are clogging the dam at the power station in West Africa.

speaker: John
en: Turbines of the power station are often shut down because of the intertwined leaves.

speaker: John
en: Dams often cease to function as a result, severely restricting the electricity generation.

speaker: John
en: Dense mats of water hyacinth also shade the surface and prevent algae growth, thus limiting the production of oxygen and threatening the survival of fish.

speaker: Arya
en: So where did water hyacinth originate from?

speaker: John
en: Water hyacinth is native to Amazon Basin in Latin America.

speaker: John
en: It has spread mainly to the tropics and subtropics throughout much of the world since the 1800s.

speaker: John
en: Later, it was introduced into Europe and Africa as an ornamental garden pond plant due to its beauty.

speaker: John
en: Flowers of this plant were being sold in local markets in African countries as a source of income for women.

speaker: John
en: Since the 1900s, this weed has infested lakes, rivers and creeks of the delta areas in West Africa.

speaker: Arya
en: I guess whoever introduced this plant had never expected the disastrous impact it would have on the local ecological environment.

speaker: Arya
en: What kind of habitat does water hyacinth thrive most in?

speaker: John
en: Well, it has been discovered that the nutrient-rich environment contributes significantly to the rapid proliferation of this weed, which obtains its nutrients directly from the water.

speaker: John
en: This is normally compounded with a decrease of nutrients along the river banks.

speaker: Arya
en: Why is that?

speaker: John
en: The main reason for soil nutrient loss is that local residents cut down trees.

speaker: John
en: Excessive deforestation accelerates soil erosion, which indirectly compounds this issue.

speaker: John
en: Without enough trees to retain nutrients in the soil, frequent rain might partly aggravate this by carrying some of the nutrients into the waterways.

speaker: Arya
en: Are there any effective measures to control or even eradicate this weed?

speaker: John
en: Yes, a number of approaches have been widely applied throughout the world.

speaker: John
en: Among them, biological control is the most widely favoured long-term control method by introducing a type of insect that feeds on water hyacinth.

speaker: John
en: So far, Nigeria has used this method for 6 months.

speaker: John
en: However, water hyacinth won't be removed immediately.

speaker: John
en: The side effects of this method on local ecology will remain unforeseen for years because it takes time for this external insect species to reach a density sufficient enough to affect ecological development.

speaker: Arya
en: Then what about other approaches?

speaker: Arya
en: Is there a quicker way to control it?

speaker: John
en: Well, the mechanical removal of water hyacinth is seen as the best short-term solution.

speaker: John
en: This option includes harvesting plants and in-site cutting.

speaker: John
en: This method can immediately open physical space for fish and boat traffic.

speaker: John
en: It is, however, inefficient for workers to process extensive areas, and costly to use large cutting and dredging equipment.

speaker: John
en: Ironically, it costs even more to dispose of this plant than to remove it.

speaker: Arya
en: Can we make use of this weed and turn it into something good?

speaker: John
en: Now scientists think that water hyacinth can be useful.

speaker: John
en: The plant itself, although more than 95% water, has a fibrous tissue and a high energy and protein content, and can be used for a variety of useful applications.

speaker: Arya
en: So what do people do with water hyacinth that is initially removed?

speaker: John
en: First, the cut-down water hyacinth is left to dry under the sun for a few days before being mixed with ash and animal manure.

speaker: John
en: The mixture can be used as alternative soil rather than fertiliser.

speaker: John
en: It can grow crops with increased yield.

speaker: Arya
en: That sounds interesting.

speaker: John
en: Mushroom farmers can benefit from water hyacinth as well.

speaker: John
en: Using the mixture mentioned earlier, mushrooms generally grow much faster so that farmers can harvest mushrooms sooner, which enables them to make money faster.

speaker: Arya
en: I see.

speaker: Arya
en: It can be used to grow oyster and straw mushrooms, right?

speaker: John
en: Yes.

speaker: John
en: In India, attempts have been made to grow these types of edible mushrooms organically utilizing water hyacinth.

speaker: John
en: These mushrooms provide sufficient nutritional values including minerals and high-quality protein which are essential to people's level of fitness.

speaker: Arya
en: Right.

speaker: Arya
en: Are there any other measures taken to utilise water hyacinth?

speaker: John
en: In South-east Asia, people feed cows using chopped water hyacinth mixed with other vegetables.

speaker: John
en: Then the waste from cows is treated to produce methane gas, which can be used as fuel for cooking, lighting or powering an engine.

speaker: John
en: And with global warming getting worse, developing alternative sources of energy will be more urgent than ever.

speaker: Arya
en: Maybe in the future people will love water hyacinth instead of hating it.

speaker: John
en: Yes, that's very likely if we can make full use of this weed.
